items.title: Bayes' theorem
items.description: Naive Bayes applies P(y|x) = P(x|y)P(y)/P(x); the conditional independence assumption is how we make P(x|y) tractable inside that formula.

items.title: Likelihood functions
items.description: Each factor P(xᵢ|y) is a likelihood component; the Likelihood Functions topic treats P(data|parameter) more generally—here parameters are implicit in the per-class per-feature distributions.

items.title: Random variables
items.description: Features xᵢ and label y are random variables; PMFs and PDFs from Random Variables supply the P(xᵢ|y) terms multiplied under the independence assumption.

items.title: Gaussian Naive Bayes
items.description: Gaussian NB instantiates each P(xᵢ|y) as a univariate normal density, then applies the product rule from this topic for continuous features.

items.title: Multinomial Naive Bayes
items.description: Multinomial/Bernoulli NB models word-count or binary features with discrete distributions, using the same ∏ P(xᵢ|y) factorization for text classification.

items.title: Classification vs regression
items.description: Naive Bayes is a classification method: it outputs discrete class labels via argmax over P(y|x). The independence assumption does not apply to standard regression, which models continuous targets directly.
